title: Aftermath - Let's Play - Part One
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=d09nbbfOIA8

Daniel (@dungeondive) kicks off the playthrough proper. The first main mission is *Raid on Enjo* — the *dungeon of Enjo* turns out to be a battered old vending machine, half its sign rusted away, and the prize inside is a single bag of onions noodles (a Funyuns analogue) that Patch the colony elder believes will keep the colony fed for a week.

Grumpel and Mesaiya leave Abigaile Lane and step into the city's first encounter card: a hostile gecko warband. The opening battle is the video's spine, and it doubles as a tour of Aftermath's core systems — colour-coded action cards that simultaneously move you, attack, and defend; opposed skill tests where the *black* die scales against you; a Threat Track that gives the enemies their turn the moment their threat-card count hits parity with the on-board enemies; and a Calamity Track that triggers a scenario-specific bad thing on a separate die roll. Combat resolves through *cards initiate, dice modify* — every check is an opposed skill test against a target, not a roll-to-hit.

Daniel has, in his own words, the worst dice luck of any campaign he can remember. By the end of turn three both heroes are at 2 HP and the gecko leader still has full health. The mission is in serious trouble — but Aftermath's *fail-forward* philosophy means a loss here isn't a campaign-ender.

----

Aftermath's combat is *cards initiate, dice modify*. Every check is an opposed skill test where the black die runs against you. Does that approach reduce dice swing for you, or just hide it behind another step?
